merged_events.event_description: Diplomatic relations and the American Legation in Lisbon were established when U.S. Minister David Humphreys presented his credentials.

evidence.quote: Diplomatic relations and the American Legation in Lisbon were established on May 13, 1791, when U.S. Minister to Portugal Col. David Humphreys presented his credentials.

merged_events.event_description: The U.S. Legation relocated from Lisbon to Rio de Janeiro following the King of Portugal's flight to Brazil during the Napoleonic Wars.

evidence.quote: During the Napoleonic Wars, the King of Portugal fled to Brazil. The U.S. legation followed and was located there from 1810-21. It closed in July 1821.

merged_events.event_description: The U.S. Legation in Rio de Janeiro closed in July 1821.

evidence.quote: The U.S. legation followed and was located there from 1810-21. It closed in July 1821.

merged_events.event_description: The U.S. Legation returned to Lisbon in 1822. Henry Dearborn, Sr. was appointed U.S. Envoy to Portugal and arrived at Lisbon before August 16, 1822, but did not report a date of presentation of credentials.

evidence.quote: In 1822 the U.S. legation returned to Lisbon, along with the King of Portugal, where it has since remained. Henry Dearborn, Sr. was appointed U.S. Envoy to Portugal on May 7, 1822, and arrived at Lisbon before August 16, 1822, but did not report a date of presentation of credentials.

merged_events.event_description: The U.S. Legation in Lisbon was elevated to Embassy status when R. Henry Norweb presented his new credentials after being promoted from Minister to Ambassador.

evidence.quote: The U.S. legation in Lisbon became an embassy on June 20, 1944, when R. Henry Norweb presented his new credentials to Lisbon after he was promoted from Minister to Ambassador.

merged_events.event_description: The U.S. Legation to Portugal (then at Rio de Janeiro) was closed in 1821.

evidence.quote: Note: The Legation to Portugal at Rio de Janeiro was closed in 1821.

merged_events.event_description: Normal diplomatic relations with Portugal interrupted following the change of government on October 5, 1910, with the new Portuguese government still unrecognized by the United States when the minister left post.

evidence.quote: Normal relations interrupted on 1910-10-05. Note: The new Government of Portugal was still unrecognized by the United States when Gage left post on November 19, 1910.

merged_events.event_description: U.S. Legation to Portugal re-established when Edwin Vernon Morgan, Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary, presented credentials to the new Portuguese government.

evidence.quote: Edwin Vernon Morgan, Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary. Appointed: 1911-05-24. Credentials presented: 1911-08-03. Left post on 1912-02-11.

matched.notes: Exact match on date (1791-05-13) and status (Legation). RDCR confirms establishment when David Humphreys presented credentials.
Exact match on date (1944-06-20) and status (Embassy). RDCR confirms elevation when R. Henry Norweb presented new credentials.

missing_from_csv.event_description: U.S. Legation relocated from Lisbon to Rio de Janeiro following the King of Portugal's flight to Brazil during the Napoleonic Wars. Status remained Legation but location changed.
missing_from_csv.notes: Medium confidence. This is a geographic relocation rather than a status change, but it marks a significant shift in the mission's location. Could be noted as a relocation event if the dataset tracks such changes.

missing_from_csv.event_description: The U.S. Legation in Rio de Janeiro (accredited to Portugal) closed in July 1821. RDCR specifies July 1821; POCOM gives only 1821.
missing_from_csv.notes: High confidence from both RDCR and POCOM. This is a clear closure event that should appear in the CSV between the 1791 establishment and the 1944 elevation, representing a gap in diplomatic representation.

missing_from_csv.event_description: The U.S. Legation returned to Lisbon in 1822 along with the King of Portugal. Henry Dearborn, Sr. was appointed Envoy and arrived before August 16, 1822.
missing_from_csv.notes: Medium confidence. RDCR gives only the year 1822 for the return. No specific credential presentation date is reported. This re-establishment should follow the 1821 closure in the CSV.

missing_from_csv.event_description: Normal diplomatic relations with Portugal interrupted following the change of government (revolution) on October 5, 1910. The new Portuguese government was unrecognized by the U.S. when the minister left post.
missing_from_csv.notes: High confidence from POCOM. This interruption in relations is not reflected in the CSV. Represents a period where the U.S. did not recognize the new Portuguese republic.

missing_from_csv.event_description: U.S. Legation to Portugal re-established when Edwin Vernon Morgan presented credentials as Envoy Extraordinary and Minister Plenipotentiary to the new Portuguese government on August 3, 1911.
missing_from_csv.notes: High confidence from POCOM. This re-establishment of the legation following the 1910 interruption is not in the CSV. The credential presentation date marks the resumption of normal diplomatic relations with the Portuguese Republic.
